articles.title: Unspent housing funds show accountability isn't optional in Portland's next budget
key_findings: $100+ million in housing funds went unbudgeted for years without City oversight
Auditor requests budget cuts limited to 3% vs citywide 10%
Upcoming audits: 911/emergency comms, police body cameras, gun violence prevention, Portland Solutions (homelessness)
articles.relevance: HIGH — Directly impacts housing and fiscal dashboards. $100M unspent housing funds is a major accountability data point. Upcoming audit topics overlap with safety and homelessness dashboards.

articles.title: Arts Tax: City needs to make improvements to deliver on voter approved arts education and grants commitments
key_findings: Arts Tax generates ~$11.2M annually
City overpaid 5 of 6 districts $8K-$1.3M using wrong salary calculations
Could have disbursed $1.3M more in grants with corrected calculations
Only 5% of funds target underserved communities; 95% goes to general operating support
Wide variation across schools in arts instruction time and teacher-to-student ratios
12 recommendations issued
articles.relevance: HIGH — Directly relevant to education dashboard (arts funding across districts) and fiscal/accountability (tax revenue mismanagement). Cross-references our 6-district education data.

articles.title: Accessory Short-Term Rentals: Changes in City rules and enforcement methods needed to increase equity and effectiveness
key_findings: Portland's first-time violation fine of $27,513 is 27x higher than any other city surveyed
55% of operators fined $10K+ may be non-white, immigrants, or LGBTQ+
2024 advertising rule increased citations 9% but fines 112%
Type B permits cost $9,005 (23x more than Type A's $400)
90%+ of listings hold Type A permits, but 62% of regional housing has 3+ bedrooms
Complaint-based enforcement yields citations in only 46% of cases
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — Housing policy/enforcement equity. Useful context for housing dashboard but not a primary data source.

articles.title: Climate Justice: Clear direction, strategies, and transparency needed to follow through on the City's climate commitments
key_findings: 79% of 47 Climate Emergency Workplan actions remain ongoing, 13% achieved, 9% delayed
~25% of planned actions have staffing/funding gap exceeding $500K
City faces $93M general fund shortfall for FY 2025-26
21% of actions lack clear timeframes, 26% lack cost estimates
100% of actions fail to identify external partners or define their roles
No specific climate adaptation targets despite heat, wildfire, flooding risks
5 recommendations issued
articles.relevance: CRITICAL — We already built the Climate Accountability Platform tracking these exact 47 workplan actions. This audit is the source document. The 79%/13%/9% split should be reflected in our data.

articles.title: Q4 2025 lobbying and political consultant disclosure reports
key_findings: Q4 2025 lobbying reports now available
articles.relevance: LOW — Lobbying transparency data. Could feed into accountability dashboard but not a priority.

articles.title: Portland City Archives collaborates with Don't Shoot PDX on 'Portland in Black'
key_findings: Community archiving partnership for Black Portland history
articles.relevance: LOW — Community engagement story, not data-driven.

articles.title: Parks Fiscal Management: Systemwide goals and sustainability strategies needed to ensure parks for future generations
key_findings: 86% of Parks assets in poor or very bad condition
Maintenance backlog estimated $550-800 million
Parks manages 11,677 acres
Only 10-20% of asset lifetime costs from construction; 80-90% from maintenance
5 reviewed projects generated ~$4.7M in unfunded annual maintenance costs
6 recommendations issued, all agreed to
articles.relevance: HIGH — Quality of life dashboard tracks parks. The 86% poor condition and $550-800M backlog are critical data points. Also relevant to fiscal dashboard (unfunded maintenance).

articles.title: 2024 Audit Impact Report: Prioritization needed to implement outstanding audit recommendations
key_findings: 49% implementation rate for recommendations (5-year monitoring period)
Down from 51% in 2022
National average is 64% among peer cities
88% response rate from city leadership (up from 77%)
Top barrier: prioritization (35 recommendations stalled)
Second barrier: coordination (19 recommendations stalled)
Unimplemented recommendations disproportionately affect vulnerable populations
articles.relevance: CRITICAL — This IS the accountability dashboard. The 49% implementation rate and comparison to 64% national average should be front-and-center.

articles.title: Asset Management: Strategic and coordinated approach needed to take on City's long-standing issues
key_findings: Over $1 billion annually needed for infrastructure maintenance (2023)
City owns $74+ billion in assets across major infrastructure bureaus
Nearly 25% of assets in poor/very poor condition (2021)
Parks projects face potential 20% asset failure within 15 years
Most infrastructure built mid-1900s, near/beyond useful life
articles.relevance: HIGH — Infrastructure is a fiscal dashboard topic. $1B+ annual maintenance need vs actual spending is a major accountability gap.

key_findings: Annual overview of ombudsman activities
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — General accountability context.

articles.relevance: LOW — Specific grant program issue.

articles.title: Ombudsman's review finds City has partly implemented recommendations to reduce harm of complaint-driven property maintenance enforcement
key_findings: Complaint-based enforcement system continues negatively affecting vulnerable homeowners
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — Housing equity issue.

articles.title: Civic Life's response to grantee non-compliance and poor communication resulted in waste and inefficiency
key_findings: Wasteful spending from poor communication and inadequate grant management
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — Accountability/government efficiency.

articles.title: 2023 Audit Impact Report: Leadership on coordination, prioritization, and decision-making most needed
key_findings: Less than half of audit recommendations implemented by 2023
Implementation rate below comparable municipalities
Rates varied significantly by service area
articles.relevance: HIGH — Predecessor to the 2024 report. Tracks accountability over time.

articles.title: Exit Poll - Portland voters were well informed on ranked-choice voting
key_findings: Most Portland voters understood ranked-choice voting
Outreach gaps for District 1 and communities of color
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — Accountability/elections dashboard context.

articles.title: Vision Zero: PBOT needs to systematically evaluate whether safety projects reduce traffic deaths
key_findings: Traffic fatalities rose to 69 in 2023 despite Vision Zero (adopted 2016)
70% of fatalities in low-light conditions (2019-2022)
65% of safety spending focused on high-equity areas (2017-2021)
22% of fatalities in low-income/BIPOC communities on streets outside High Crash Network
Speed cameras not installed as promised
Transportation wallet app delayed 32 months then cancelled
articles.relevance: CRITICAL — Safety dashboard. Traffic deaths rising despite Vision Zero is a key accountability story. The 69 deaths figure and equity disparities should be in our data.

articles.title: Technology Purchasing: Citywide strategy and guidance needed to address problems
key_findings: $63 million in annual technology spending reviewed
No citywide technology purchasing strategy exists
Delays ranging 1-32 months across 7 case studies
Transportation wallet app delayed 32 months then cancelled
Can't track spending with historically disadvantaged businesses despite 20% goal
1 in 5 Portlanders speak another language at home; language access inconsistent
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — Fiscal/accountability. Technology waste is a spending story.

articles.title: Predatory towing harmed residents of low-income housing complexes
key_findings: Towing practices caused financial/emotional harm to vulnerable, predominantly Latino residents
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — Housing/equity story.

articles.title: Inclusionary Housing: Housing Bureau should improve Program goals, support, and monitoring
key_findings: 566 affordable apartments built across 78 buildings (as of April 2023)
1,157 additional units anticipated
68% of built units in high-opportunity neighborhoods
Only 23% are family-sized units (2+ bedrooms)
44% of 3-bedroom units vacant 4-15 months after opening
Bureau 2 years behind on compliance reviews (May 2023)
$5M+ in fees collected but used only for operating costs, not building housing
Black renting families' median income often insufficient for program income-to-rent ratios
articles.relevance: HIGH — Housing dashboard. The inclusionary housing pipeline (566 built, 1,157 planned) and equity gaps are critical data.

articles.title: Joint Office of Homeless Services: Sustained focus on shelter system needed
key_findings: Family shelters: 300-350 families waiting for ~100 beds (1-6 month waits)
Adult shelters: waitlists of 20-100+ people per site
Shelter capacity grew from 521 beds (2015) to 2,053 (2022) but demand exceeds supply
Only 25% of shelter exits reached permanent housing (FY 2023)
More exits returned to homelessness (1,287) than housed (1,136)
Only 3 of 18 shelters met exit-to-housing goals
Black residents: 7% of population but 17% of homeless population
Indigenous peoples: 3% of population but 13% of homeless population
7 recommendations, all agreed to
articles.relevance: CRITICAL — Homelessness dashboard. Shelter capacity, waitlists, housing outcomes, and racial disparities are exactly the flow-through pipeline data we discussed with Quinn Colling.

articles.title: Fraud Hotline Report: Civic Life's mismanagement of Kenton Firehouse was wasteful
key_findings: Waste and inefficiency from absent formal agreements and bypassed financial policies

articles.title: Joint Office of Homeless Services: contract managers have more training but role conflicts remain
key_findings: Progress on training but separation of duties not implemented
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — Homelessness accountability context.

articles.title: Portland Fire & Rescue's Community Health Division programs need guidance and leadership
key_findings: Management gaps in community health programs; improved goals and oversight recommended
articles.relevance: MEDIUM — Safety/emergency services.

key_findings: Fire Bureau communication gaps at affordable housing property

key_findings: Inequitable towing rates and gaps in police tow contract coverage
